title: Evangelion and Genre
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by Ark » Sat Nov 15, 2008 1:27 pm I apologise if this has been recently discussed but I'd like to know how people think Eva should best be described. I've been reading a lot about the history of Science Fiction lately and it would seem that Eva doesn't really qualify as part of that catergory despite how it's usually described. There's nothing really speculative about it which is what Science Fiction is primarily meant to do. How would you described it?

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Sat Nov 15, 2008 2:50 pm Science Fiction, Fantasy, Psychological, Drama. Something can have multiple genre, and all four sum it up pretty well.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Nov 15, 2008 4:10 pm Don't forget the "giant robot". I think that's considered a genre -- in Japan, anyway.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by jim_loves_randy » Sat Nov 15, 2008 6:00 pm Ark wrote: I apologise if this has been recently discussed but I'd like to know how people think Eva should best be described. I've been reading a lot about the history of Science Fiction lately and it would seem that Eva doesn't really qualify as part of that catergory despite how it's usually described. There's nothing really speculative about it which is what Science Fiction is primarily meant to do. How would you described it?

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by Ark » Sat Nov 15, 2008 7:06 pm jim_loves_randy wrote: There's a ton of speculation in Eva. For instance, is the next step of evolution for man a unification of all into one being? And the existence of a God and the fruit of life and knowledge and primordial soup and stuff.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by V » Sat Nov 15, 2008 8:10 pm Ark wrote: I apologise if this has been recently discussed but I'd like to know how people think Eva should best be described. I've been reading a lot about the history of Science Fiction lately and it would seem that Eva doesn't really qualify as part of that catergory despite how it's usually described. There's nothing really speculative about it which is what Science Fiction is primarily meant to do. How would you described it?

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by jim_loves_randy » Sun Nov 16, 2008 3:23 am V wrote: Instrumentality IS NOT the "next step" for humanity: it's Seele trying to fight back into the Garden of Eden, as it were, and become living Gods. It's not the next step, it was Seele giving up on individuality. Just because Seele, the villains of the series, say something is an "advance" doesnt' exactly make it so. The point of the series is that Shinji ultimately rejects Instrumentality, to embrace "real" life despite its pain.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Nov 16, 2008 3:37 am jim_loves_randy wrote: And who the hell decided the Angels needed an origin?

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by Ark » Sun Nov 16, 2008 8:54 am V wrote: In my personal experience, I've discovered that long ago, Hard Science Fiction proponents decided that "our way is the only way" they try as hard as they can to convince other people that "science fiction is about speculation, about technology or how we'll live in the future" really, this is just their refusal to see anything else but their own domain.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Mon Nov 17, 2008 2:44 am Don't forget the "giant robot". I think that's considered a genre -- in Japan, anyway.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by Evangelion217 » Mon Nov 17, 2008 7:57 am I've been reading a lot about the history of Science Fiction lately and it would seem that Eva doesn't really qualify as part of that catergory despite how it's usually described. There's nothing really speculative about it which is what Science Fiction is primarily meant to do. How would you described it?

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by Evangelion217 » Mon Nov 17, 2008 8:02 am Anyway in terms of Eva I'd say it doesn't really fit into any of those descriptions (if that is what you're saying)

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by V » Mon Nov 17, 2008 9:12 am jim_loves_randy wrote: V wrote: Instrumentality IS NOT the "next step" for humanity: it's Seele trying to fight back into the Garden of Eden, as it were, and become living Gods. It's not the next step, it was Seele giving up on individuality. Just because Seele, the villains of the series, say something is an "advance" doesnt' exactly make it so. The point of the series is that Shinji ultimately rejects Instrumentality, to embrace "real" life despite its pain.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Mon Nov 17, 2008 10:10 am The talk about the FAR and other material comes from this- http://www.evageeks.org/FGP/Classified_Information Supposedly based off extensive interviews with Hideaki Anno. "Classified Information" indeed...

[]:             Quote Post by Sharaz Destler » Mon Nov 17, 2008 10:52 am Reichu wrote: Don't forget the "giant robot". I think that's considered a genre -- in Japan, anyway.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by Ark » Mon Nov 17, 2008 12:01 pm Evangelion217 wrote: Especially since it treats depressiong as allegory.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Mon Nov 17, 2008 4:13 pm More depression, obviously.

[]:             Quote Post by jim_loves_randy » Mon Nov 17, 2008 6:52 pm Well, fuck me. There's so much I don't know about Eva :(

[]:             Quote Post by zlink64 » Thu Nov 20, 2008 3:17 pm it ' s a drama first then a sci-fi/fanasy

[]: Re: Evangelion and Genre             Quote Post by Evangelion217 » Mon Nov 24, 2008 7:17 am An allegory for what?
